elements.sent-2411-002: Determining the maximal density $m_1(\bbbr^2)$ of planar sets without unit distances is a fundamental problem in combinatorial geometry.
elements.sent-2411-003: This paper investigates lower bounds for this quantity.
elements.sent-2411-004: We introduce a novel approach to estimating $m_1(\bbbr^2)$ by reformulating the problem as a Maximal Independent Set (MIS) problem on graphs constructed from flat torus, focusing on periodic sets with respect to two non-collinear vectors.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our experimental results supported by theoretical justifications of proposed method demonstrate that for a sufficiently wide range of parameters this approach does not improve the known lower bound $0.22936 \le m_1(\bbbr^2)$.
elements.sent-2411-006: The best discrete sets found are approximations of Croft's construction.
elements.sent-2411-007: In addition, several open source software packages for MIS problem are compared on this task.
elements.sent-2411-008: In the 1960s, Leo Moser \cite{croft1967incidence,erdos1985 raised a natural question in combinatorial geometry area: How dense can a set in $\bbbr^d$ be if it contains no pairs of points being unit-distance apart? This problem, belonging to the field of combinatorial geometry known as the combinatorics of unit distances, is closely related to the well-known Hadwiger–Nelson problem \cite{Soifer2016TheHP , proposed by Nelson in 1950.}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Nelson asked: What is the minimum number of colors required to color the Euclidean plane $\bbbr^d$ such that no two points at unit distance apart are identically colored? This question has gained renewed interest in recent years following de Grey's groundbreaking discovery \cite{degrey2018chromatic that the plane is not 4-colorable.}
elements.sent-2411-010: The PolyMath16 project \cite{PolyMath16 has also contributed significantly to ongoing research in this area.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Notably, any proper plane coloring requires that each color class avoids unit distances.
elements.sent-2411-012: This observation motivates the focus of this paper.
elements.sent-2411-013: A graph with vertices in $\bbbr^d$ is called a unit distance graph if two vertices are adjacent if and only if they are at Euclidean distance 1 apart.
elements.sent-2411-014: The unit distance graph of $\bbbr^d$ is obtained by taking all points of $\bbbr^d$ as vertices, and connecting two vertices by an edge if and only if the corresponding points are at unit distance apart.
elements.sent-2411-015: The chromatic number of this graph is denoted by $\chi(\bbbr^d)$.
elements.sent-2411-016: A set $A \in \bbbr^d$ is called 1-avoiding if $A$ is the independent set in some unit distance graph with vertices in $\bbbr^d$.
elements.sent-2411-018: The upper density of $A$, denoted by $\overline{\delta(A)}$, is defined as \begin{equation}\overline{\delta(A)} = \displaystyle\lim\sup_{R \rightarrow \infty}\frac{\lambda_d(A \cap B_d(x, R))}{\lambda_d(B_d(x, R))},\end{equation} where $\lambda_d$ is the $d$-dimensional Lebesgue measure, $B_d(x, R)$ indicates the $d$-dimensional ball of radius $R$ centered at $x$, and $x \in \bbbr^d$ is fixed.
elements.sent-2411-019: The definition is valid since the upper density is known to be independent of the choice of $x \in \bbbr^d$.
elements.sent-2411-020: Let $m_1(\bbbr^d)$ denote the supremum of the upper densities of all 1-avoiding measurable sets in $\bbbr^d$: \begin{equation}m_1(\bbbr^d) = \sup \left\{ \overline{\delta(A)}: A \in \bbbr^d is 1-avoiding and measurable \right\}\end{equation} Estimating $m_1(\bbbr^2)$ was posed and popularized by Leo Moser (see Problem 25 in \cite{Moser1966 ) and Pal Erdős.}
elements.sent-2411-021: For over 50 years, various researchers have tried to improve the upper and lower bounds of this value.
elements.sent-2411-022: The simplest non-trivial lower bound can be obtained by placing open circular discs of radius $1/2$ on a regular hexagonal lattice generated by two vectors of length $2$ forming an angle $\pi/3$.
elements.sent-2411-023: This configuration creates a set that avoids distances of $1$, with a density of $\pi/\left(8\sqrt{3 \right) \approx 0.2267$.}
elements.sent-2411-024: A slight improvement upon this lower bound was proposed by Croft \cite{croft1967incidence in 1967, establishing that $m_1(\bbbr^2) \ge 0.22936...$.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Croft's construction is based on the shape of a ``tortoise'', which is the intersection of an open disc of radius $1/2$ and an open regular hexagon of height $x < 1$.
elements.sent-2411-026: By placing copies of this tortoise at each point of a regular hexagonal lattice with basis vectors of length $1 + x$, a 1-avoiding planar set is created.
elements.sent-2411-027: Optimizing the value of $x$ reveals that the maximal density is achieved with $x^* = 0.96533...$.
elements.sent-2411-028: It is worth noting that this construction is periodic.
elements.sent-2411-029: Despite its apparent simplicity, no further improvements to the $m_1(\bbbr^2)$ lower bound have been suggested since 1967.
elements.sent-2411-030: However, upper bound of $m_1(\bbbr^2)$ has been improved several times over these years.
elements.sent-2411-031: Regarding the maximal density in the whole plane, Pal Erdős stated in \cite{erdos1985 : ``It seems very likely that $m_1(\bbbr^2)$ is less than 1/4''.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Surprisingly, this conjecture was proven after almost 40 years in 2022.
elements.sent-2411-033: Gergely Ambrus and Mate Matolcsi \cite{ambrus2022 , using a combination of Fourier analytic and linear programming methods, first proved that $m_1(\bbbr^2) \le 0.25442$.}
elements.sent-2411-034: One year later, in 2023, these authors, along with colleagues, improved their estimate to $m_1(\bbbr^2) \le 0.247$, confirming Erdős's statement \cite{ambrus2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Additionally, a recent paper \cite{cohen2024 explores clustering in 1-avoiding planar sets via the linear programming approach and Fourier analysis tools previously used previously for upper bound proofs of $m_1(\bbbr^2)$.}
elements.sent-2411-036: This highlights that the study of such sets remains attractive and relevant to various scientists.
elements.sent-2411-037: Finally, according to the best existing bounds, $0.2293 \le m_1(\bbbr^2) \le 0.2470$, a significant gap between upper and lower estimates of $m_1(\bbbr^2)$ remains.
elements.sent-2411-038: The periodic structure of current constructions for the lower bound, coupled with the lack of lower bound improvements for over 50 years, motivates the exploration of 1-avoiding planar sets from the perspective of searching for optimal or sub-optimal periodic structures.
elements.sent-2411-039: This is the main goal of this paper, which proposes a parameterization of planar periodic sets and its analysis.
elements.sent-2411-040: More precisely, we study planar sets that are periodic with respect to two non-collinear vectors and explore the lower bound of $m_1(\bbbr^2)$ over this class of measurable planar sets without unit distances.
elements.sent-2411-041: Let's consider a the measurable set $A \subset \bbbr^2$ as a coloring of the plane with two colors.

elements.subsection-2411-002: Independent set solvers and machine learning for combinatorial optimization
elements.sent-2411-226: In this part of the paper, the Maximum Independent Set (MIS) problem is formally introduced, the solvers used in our analysis are described, and we discuss relevant research in the broader field of deep learning for combinatorial optimization.
elements.sent-2411-227: Given an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$, an independent set is a subset of vertices $S \subset V$ where no two vertices in $S$ are connected by an edge.
elements.sent-2411-228: Formally, for all vertices $u, v \in S$, $(u, v) \notin E$.
elements.sent-2411-229: Let each vertex $u \in V$ have an associated weight $w_u$.
elements.sent-2411-230: The Maximum Weighted Independent Set (MWIS) problem seeks to find the independent set $S$ that maximizes the sum of its vertex weights: \begin{equation}\displaystyle\arg\max_{S \in IS(G)}\sum\limits_{u \in S}w_u.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-231: The unweighted MIS problem is a special case of MWIS where all vertices have equal weights, i.e., $w_u = 1$ for all $u \in V$.
elements.sent-2411-233: According to the aim of this paper, we focus on solving the MIS problem.
elements.sent-2411-234: For the experiments in this paper the open-source independent set benchmarking suite MIS-Benchmark \cite{mis_benchmark2022 for the NP-hard Maximum Independent Set search was used.}
elements.sent-2411-235: This suite integrates several solvers into a single, easily accessible command-line interface using Anaconda \cite{Anaconda2020 and provides a unified input and output format.}
elements.sent-2411-236: The framework ensures the proper invocation of the solvers and enables running experiments on various solvers in different configurations.
elements.sent-2411-237: We consider several solvers (KaMIS, Intel-TreeSearch, DGL-TreeSearch, Learning What to Defer) integrated into MIS-Benchmark in more details.
elements.sent-2411-238: Note that despite good maximal independent set size approximations, none of these methods guarantee that the found set will be a true maximal independent set for a given graph.. KaMIS.

elements.sent-2411-244: In the influential paper \cite{Li2018_combopt , Zhuwen Li et al. (2018) propose a guided tree search algorithm to find maximum independent sets of a graph.}
elements.sent-2411-245: Their key idea is to train a graph convolutional network (GCN) \cite{KipfWelling2017_gcn , which assigns each vertex a probability of belonging to the independent set.}
elements.sent-2411-246: The algorithm then greedily and iteratively assigns vertices to the set based on these probabilities.
